statement.en: Lice lessons: using biochemical analyses of external parasites to reconstruct past host lives
statement.summary: This thesis explores how blood-feeding ectoparasites such as lice, fleas, and ticks can be used as tools to reconstruct aspects of host diet, health, and life history. Despite acting as sources of ecological and biochemical information on their hosts, ectoparasites remain underrepresented in collections-based studies. To provide context, the historical relationship between humans and their ectoparasites is reviewed, highlighting the shifts in societal perspectives alongside growing conservation concerns. This research examined the application of ectoparasites for biochemical analysis of their hosts by establishing a modern methodological testbed for stable isotope analysis to reconstruct host dietary ecology and feeding patterns, as well as conducting molecular analyses to recover host DNA

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: This is a 2026-dated thesis (future/provisional document) presenting novel methodological frameworks for parasite-based host reconstruction. The core claims are empirically testable but the work appears to be in validation phase—stable isotope methodology is established, but its application to ectoparasite biochemistry for host life history reconstruction is emerging. The thesis combines proven techniques (isotope analysis, molecular DNA recovery) with novel integrative application, making it provisional rather than established.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 寄生虫の中に宿主の時間が凝結している。
